title: N-Queens Puzzle with Rotated Board and Overcounting

statement.markdown: Given a board size n, find all configurations that place n queens on a board such that no two queens attack each other. The board is rotated, meaning that the columns and rows are swapped. However, counting each rotated configuration as a unique solution, we overcount due to the board's rotational symmetry.
statement.inputFormat: First line contains integer n, representing the size of the board. The next n lines contain the initial state of the board where 0 represents an empty cell and 1 represents a queen. The board is 0-indexed and the first cell is at the top-left corner.
statement.outputFormat: Your program should output the number of unique configurations.
statement.notes: The input board size is guaranteed to be a perfect square. The input board will never contain two queens in the same row or column, but may contain empty cells between queens in the same row.

examples.explanation:
The two valid configurations are: 
 0000
 1010
 0000
 0100
 and 
 0000
 0100
 0000
 1010

hints.text: Consider a row and column as identical if they are permutations of each other.
When swapping columns and rows, do not forget to adjust the indices of the board accordingly.

editorial.title: Editorial: Rotated Board N-Queens Puzzle
editorial.intuition: When solving the rotated board N-Queens puzzle, consider using a backtracking algorithm similar to the standard N-Queens problem. However, when swapping columns and rows, keep track of the indices to ensure correct placement and rotation.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force solution involves placing a queen in each row and checking if any two queens attack each other. However, due to the rotated symmetry, we must also consider the permutations of rows and columns.
editorial.betterSolution: To avoid overcounting due to rotational symmetry, use a data structure such as a hashmap to store the unique configurations.
editorial.optimalSolution: In the optimal solution, we use a backtracking algorithm to place queens on the rotated board. We keep track of the current row, column, and diagonal indices to ensure correct placement. When swapping columns and rows, we adjust the indices accordingly.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The solution is correct because it ensures that no two queens attack each other and correctly handles the rotational symmetry of the board.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm should include checking if any two queens in the same row or column attack each other, along with handling rotational symmetry.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly handling column and row swapping

functionSignature.cpp: void helper(vector<vector<int>>& board, int row, int n, int& count, int& uniqueCount);
functionSignature.java: void helper(int[][] board, int row, int n, int count, int uniqueCount);
functionSignature.python: def helper(self, board, row, n, count, uniqueCount):
